pain_point_label: Procurement Governance & Compliance Fragmentation Across Sites

matches.match_rationale: Invoices arriving without matching POs or requisition approvals are classic anomalous patterns relative to a compliant procurement baseline. An anomaly detection model trained on historical compliant vs. non-compliant transactions could flag unauthorized purchases — bypass of quote requirements, unapproved suppliers, missing approval chain — in real time as invoices are processed, before they clear AP. For a 500+ staff multi-site operator, this directly addresses the core pain: invisible non-compliant spend that only surfaces at audit time.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An AI layer over the AP inbox and ERP that scores each incoming invoice for compliance anomalies — missing PO reference, unregistered vendor, single-quote purchase above threshold — and routes high-risk items to the central finance team for review before payment is released.

matches.match_rationale: Procurement documents — supplier quotes, delivery receipts, invoices, PO copies — arrive as scanned PDFs, email attachments, and photos. Parsing these into structured records is a prerequisite for any compliance check: you cannot match a paper invoice to a PO if one is machine-readable and the other is an image. For schools that run on paper-heavy processes (common in PH government-funded institutions), OCR and form extraction convert scattered physical evidence into auditable digital records.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A document ingestion pipeline that automatically OCRs incoming supplier invoices, quotes, and PO copies, extracts key fields (vendor, amount, date, line items), and writes structured records to a central compliance log — giving the central finance team a searchable, audit-ready trail without manual data entry.

matches.match_rationale: Requisition approvals live in email threads, PO records are in campus-level spreadsheets, and supplier documentation is filed inconsistently across sites. Data transformation AI can normalize these semi-structured inputs — varying column names, date formats, vendor name spellings — into a single canonical procurement schema. This makes cross-site aggregation and compliance reporting feasible without a full ERP replacement, which the organization is unlikely to afford in 6-12 months.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An ETL pipeline with AI-assisted schema mapping that ingests per-campus procurement exports (spreadsheets, email exports, ERP dumps), normalizes them to a unified format, and loads them into a central compliance dashboard that tracks PO coverage rates, quote compliance, and supplier documentation status per site.

matches.match_rationale: Email threads and internal communications contain embedded requisition approvals, informal purchase authorizations, and supplier negotiations. Classifying emails by procurement intent — approved requisition, informal purchase request, supplier quote, complaint, renewal notice — enables automated extraction of approval evidence that currently exists only as unstructured email history. This directly addresses the audit finding that approval trails are 'in email threads' and cannot be produced on demand.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An email classification layer that scans the finance and campus admin inboxes, tags messages as procurement-related by type, and links them to open PO records — building a reconstructed approval audit trail from existing email history for past transactions and flagging new informal approvals in real time.

matches.match_rationale: Procurement compliance at a multi-site operator is a multi-step workflow: intake a requisition, check against policy (threshold, preferred vendor list, site-specific rules), route for approval, collect quotes, match to PO, verify invoice, archive documentation. Each step today is manual and inconsistently executed across campuses. A task automation agent can enforce this workflow end-to-end — triggering reminders, blocking payment release until compliance steps are complete, and logging every action for audit purposes.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A procurement compliance agent that acts as a workflow enforcer: when a campus submits a purchase request above threshold, the agent automatically checks vendor registration, routes for the correct approval chain based on amount and site rules, tracks quote collection, and blocks AP processing until all required documentation is attached — with full audit log export.

matches.match_rationale: At audit time, the organization must produce written compliance explanations, exception justifications, and procurement narrative reports — currently an entirely manual exercise reconstructed from scattered evidence. LLM-based report generation can auto-draft audit response packages, summarize per-site procurement compliance status, and produce board-level CFO narratives from structured procurement data, saving significant time and reducing the risk of incomplete or inconsistent audit responses.
matches.opportunity_sketch: An audit-ready report generator that, given a date range and site selection, produces a structured procurement compliance report — overall PO coverage rate, flagged exceptions with auto-drafted justifications from available context, and a supplier documentation status table — formatted for submission to government auditors.

matches.match_rationale: Finance staff and campus administrators need to answer specific compliance questions — 'Does this vendor appear on our preferred vendor list?', 'What is the approval threshold for maintenance contracts at this site?', 'Has this supplier submitted their BIR registration?' — from a fragmented corpus of policy documents, vendor files, and site-specific regulations. RAG enables a single query interface over this distributed knowledge base, replacing manual file searching across emails, shared drives, and paper files.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A procurement policy Q&A tool where campus procurement staff can ask natural language questions against the organization's policy documents, vendor registry, and site-specific compliance rules, receiving grounded, citation-backed answers that reduce policy misinterpretation — the leading cause of inadvertent non-compliance.

matches.match_rationale: Not every campus and not every purchase carries equal compliance risk — high-spend campuses, campuses with known audit history, purchases near the threshold, and single-source renewals are systematically higher risk. A risk scoring model can prioritize central finance review effort toward the transactions and sites most likely to produce audit findings, rather than requiring uniform oversight of all 500+ staff's purchasing activity.
matches.opportunity_sketch: A procurement risk scoring engine that assigns a compliance risk score to each in-flight requisition and each campus's monthly spend profile, based on factors like vendor registration status, PO coverage rate, quote compliance history, and proximity to approval thresholds — enabling the CFO to focus review effort where exposure is highest.
